title: Apply: The Collapse of Reconstruction
description: Apply your understanding of why Reconstruction collapsed in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from William W. Holden’s 1870 report to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

There exists in this State a secret, oath-bound, armed organization, which is hostile to the State government, and to the Government of the United States. Bands of these armed men ride at night through various neighborhoods, whipping and maltreating peaceable citizens, hanging some, burning churches, and breaking up schools which have been established for the colored people. These outrages are almost [[☃ definition 1]] committed on persons, white and colored, who are most devoted in their feelings and conduct to the Government of the United States.

**This excerpt best serves as evidence of which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Text source: *Reconstruction: Voices from America’s First Great Struggle for Racial Equality,* 1870.

choices.content: The rise in racial violence in the southern states during Reconstruction
The continued practice of slavery in the southern states, despite abolition
The passage of laws in the South that allowed for race-based discrimination
The start of secession from the federal government after the election of 1860

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from William W. Holden’s 1870 report to President Ulysses S. Grant.**

There exists in this State a secret, oath-bound, armed organization, which is hostile to the State government, and to the Government of the United States. Bands of these armed men ride at night through various neighborhoods, whipping and maltreating peaceable citizens, hanging some, burning churches, and breaking up schools which have been established for the colored people. These outrages are almost [[☃ definition 1]] committed on persons, white and colored, who are most devoted in their feelings and conduct to the Government of the United States.

**The violence described in the excerpt led to the passage of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Text source: *Reconstruction: Voices from America’s First Great Struggle for Racial Equality,* 1870.

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment
The Enforcement Acts
The Civil Rights Act of 1866
The Compromise of 1877

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in the *Civil Rights Cases* (1883). It was written by Justice Joseph P. Bradley.**

We must not forget that the [[☃ definition 1]] and scope of the Thirteenth and Fourteenth amendments are different: the former simply abolished slavery; the latter prohibited the States from [[☃ definition 2]] the privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States, from depriving them of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, and from denying to any the equal protection of the laws. The amendments are different, and the powers of Congress under them are different. What Congress has power to do under one, it may not have power to do under the other. . . 

Can the act of a mere individual, the owner of the inn, the public [[☃ definition 3]] or place of amusement, refusing the accommodation, be justly regarded as imposing any badge of slavery or servitude upon the applicant, or only as inflicting an ordinary civil injury, properly [[☃ definition 5]] by the laws of the State and presumably subject to [[☃ definition 4]] by those laws until the contrary appears?

After giving to these questions all the consideration which their importance demands, we are forced to the conclusion that such an act of refusal has nothing to do with slavery or involuntary servitude, and that, if it is violative of any right of the party, his redress is to be sought under the laws of the State . . .

**Which statement best summarizes Justice Bradley’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: able to be recognized; able to be tried by a court

choices.content: Congress can use the Fourteenth Amendment to prohibit private individuals from committing race-based discrimination.
Congress has no power to interfere with state laws that discriminate based on a person’s race.
Congress does not have the power under the Thirteenth and Fourteenth Amendments to ban race-based discrimination by private individuals.
Congress cannot interfere with a state’s voting laws.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in the *Civil Rights Cases* (1883). It was written by Justice Joseph P. Bradley.**

We must not forget that the [[☃ definition 1]] and scope of the Thirteenth and Fourteenth amendments are different: the former simply abolished slavery; the latter prohibited the States from [[☃ definition 2]] the privileges or immunities of citizens of the United States, from depriving them of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, and from denying to any the equal protection of the laws. The amendments are different, and the powers of Congress under them are different. What Congress has power to do under one, it may not have power to do under the other. . . 

Can the act of a mere individual, the owner of the inn, the public [[☃ definition 3]] or place of amusement, refusing the accommodation, be justly regarded as imposing any badge of slavery or servitude upon the applicant, or only as inflicting an ordinary civil injury, properly [[☃ definition 4]] by the laws of the State and presumably subject to [[☃ definition 5]] by those laws until the contrary appears?

After giving to these questions all the consideration which their importance demands, we are forced to the conclusion that such an act of refusal has nothing to do with slavery or involuntary servitude, and that, if it is violative of any right of the party, his redress is to be sought under the laws of the State . . .

**The Supreme Court’s reasoning in this case led to which law being struck down?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: able to be recognized; able to be tried by a court

choices.content: The Freedmen’s Bureau Act of 1865
The Civil Rights Act of 1866
The Reconstruction Acts of 1867
The Civil Rights Act of 1875

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Court of Arbitration,” an 1877 article in the *Chicago Tribune.***

The country demands peace; it cares nothing personally for HAYES OR TILDEN, or this or that party; and when the means are offered for a prompt, final, and peaceful [[☃ definition 2]] of the whole difficulty by a majority of the Judges of the Supreme Court, it will hold every man and every party guilty of a great national wrong who shall defeat such adjudication. . . .

The politicians, the [[☃ definition 3]], strikers and blowers in office and out of office, stand in the way. They want office and plunder, and they prefer even war to a denial of their wants. Woe be unto those who overlook the suffering interests of the whole people and listen to the clamor of the howling mob of [[☃ definition 4]].

**Which event was the author of this article responding to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: people given official jobs in exchange for supporting a politician
those who seek to personally benefit from a party's success in an election

choices.content: The presidential election of 1860
The *Slaughter-House Cases*
The presidential election of 1876
The *Civil Rights Cases*

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “The Court of Arbitration,” an 1877 article in the *Chicago Tribune.***

The country demands peace; it cares nothing personally for HAYES OR TILDEN, or this or that party; and when the means are offered for a prompt, final, and peaceful [[☃ definition 1]] of the whole difficulty by a majority of the Judges of the Supreme Court, it will hold every man and every party guilty of a great national wrong who shall defeat such adjudication. . . .

The politicians, the [[☃ definition 2]], strikers and blowers in office and out of office, stand in the way. They want office and plunder, and they prefer even war to a denial of their wants. Woe be unto those who overlook the suffering interests of the whole people and listen to the clamor of the howling mob of [[☃ definition 3]].

**How was the conflict described in the excerpt resolved?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: people given official jobs in exchange for supporting a politician
those who seek to personally benefit from a party's success in an election

choices.content: Tilden won the presidency, and the South threatened to secede again.
Tilden won the presidency after the Supreme Court stepped in and ruled in his favor.
Hayes won the presidency after the military forced a second election.
Hayes won the presidency, and federal troops were removed from the South.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Around the World With General Grant*, published in 1879. In this excerpt, author John Russell Young is quoting Grant’s own words.**

Looking back, over the whole policy of reconstruction, it seems to me that the wisest thing would have been to have continued for some time the military rule....Military rule would have been just to all, to the negro who wanted freedom, the white man who wanted protection, the Northern man who wanted Union. As State after State showed a willingness to come into the Union, not on their own terms but upon ours, I would have admitted them. This would have made universal suffrage unnecessary, and I think a mistake was made about suffrage. It was unjust to the negro to throw upon him the responsibilities of citizenship, and expect him to be on even terms with his white neighbor. It was unjust to the North. In giving the South negro suffrage, we have given the old slave-holders forty votes in the electoral college. They keep those votes, but [[☃ definition 1]] the negroes.

**Which statement best describes President Grant’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: deprive of legal rights, such as the right to vote

choices.content: Reconstruction was a success because it gave Black people full citizenship.
Reconstruction was a success because the South rejoined the Union.
Reconstruction was a failure because many of its goals were not met.
Reconstruction was a failure because it did not prevent the South from seceding.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *Around the World With General Grant*, published in 1879. In this excerpt, author John Russell Young is quoting Grant’s own words.**

Looking back, over the whole policy of reconstruction, it seems to me that the wisest thing would have been to have continued for some time the military rule....Military rule would have been just to all, to the negro who wanted freedom, the white man who wanted protection, the Northern man who wanted Union. As State after State showed a willingness to come into the Union, not on their own terms but upon ours, I would have admitted them. This would have made universal suffrage unnecessary, and I think a mistake was made about suffrage. It was unjust to the negro to throw upon him the responsibilities of citizenship, and expect him to be on even terms with his white neighbor. It was unjust to the North. In giving the South negro suffrage, we have given the old slave-holders forty votes in the electoral college. They keep those votes, but [[☃ definition 1]] the negroes.

**Grant suggests that the southern states “keep those [electoral] votes, but disenfranchise the negroes.” What was one strategy southern states used to achieve this goal?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: deprive of legal rights, such as the right to vote

choices.content: Requiring Black voters to pass literacy tests
Passing laws that limited state and local elections to White voters
Segregating voting booths based on race
Requiring Black voters show photo IDs before voting

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**Which statement best expresses Nast’s message in this political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: Thomas Nast, “Shall We Call Home Our Troops? ‘We intend to beat the negro in the battle of life & defeat means one thing—EXTERMINATION.’” *Harper’s Weekly,* [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/cph.3a03175/), 1875.

options.alt: A political cartoon depicting a Union soldier using his weapon to stop a Southern man from attacking a Black man who is cowering in fear. The soldier is standing under a sign that reads “Office of U.S. Marshall.” The Southern man holds a gun in one hand and a whip in the other. There is a crowd of angry men behind him with signs that read, “We intend to beat the negro in the battle of life & defeat means one thing—EXTERMINATION. Birmingham Alabama News,” “KKK,” and “Call Home Your Troops.” The Black man holds a scroll that has “Equal Rights” printed on it. He is cowering on the steps of the U.S. Marshal’s office.
options.caption: “Shall We Call Home Our Troops?” by Thomas Nast, 1875. Note: The Black man holds a scroll that has “Equal Rights” printed on it.

choices.content: The U.S. military is not doing enough to protect the equal rights of Black people in the South, leading to violations of the Reconstruction Amendments.
If the United States removes its military presence from the South, White supremacists will take away the rights of Black Americans.
The U.S. military’s presence is interfering with democratic processes and elections in the South, violating Southerners’ rights.
If the United States continues its Reconstruction efforts, those efforts will foster resentment from White Southerners, leading to another civil war.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**This political cartoon could be used as evidence of which development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: Thomas Nast, “Shall We Call Home Our Troops? ‘We intend to beat the negro in the battle of life & defeat means one thing—EXTERMINATION.’” *Harper’s Weekly,* [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/cph.3a03175/), 1875.

options.alt: A political cartoon depicting a Union soldier using his weapon to stop a Southern man from attacking a Black man who is cowering in fear. The soldier is standing under a sign that reads “Office of U.S. Marshall.” The Southern man holds a gun in one hand and a whip in the other. There is a crowd of angry men behind him with signs that read, “We intend to beat the negro in the battle of life & defeat means one thing—EXTERMINATION. Birmingham Alabama News,” “KKK,” and “Call Home Your Troops.” The Black man holds a scroll that has “Equal Rights” printed on it. He is cowering on the steps of the U.S. Marshal’s office.
options.caption: “Shall We Call Home Our Troops?” by Thomas Nast, 1875. Note: The Black man holds a scroll that has “Equal Rights” printed on it.

choices.content: The rise of racial violence committed by White supremacist groups
The increasing support for secession by White Southerners before the Civil War
The failure of Reconstruction following the Compromise of 1877
The impact of the Supreme Court’s decision in the *Civil Rights Cases* (1883)

assessment_items.question_content:
**This article from *The Augusta (Georgia) Constitutionalist* quotes Rutherford B. Hayes’s speech after the 1876 presidential election, when Hayes believed he had been defeated by Democrat Samuel J. Tilden.**

I don’t care for myself; and the party, yes, and the country, too, can stand it; but I do care for the poor colored men of the South. I do not fear that business will be greatly disturbed by Mr. Tilden’s election—trade governs itself. . . . As soon as they recover from the [[☃ definition 1]] of defeat business will, I think, go on as usual. . . . If any part of the country suffers or remains at a stand still it will be the South under the new regime. . . . But as I said before, I do pity the poor black men of the South. The result will be that the Southern people will practically treat the constitutional amendments as [[☃ definition 2]], and then the colored man’s fate will be worse than when he was in slavery with a [[☃ definition 3]] master to look after his interests. That is the only reason I regret the news as it is. 

**Which statement best summarizes the fear that Hayes expresses in the excerpt?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: things that have no legal authority or force

choices.content: The economy of the South will be greatly affected by the results of the election, as Tilden will not protect trade.
Southerners will secede from the Union again if Tilden wins the presidency, as he aims to strengthen Reconstruction measures.
The governments in the South will collapse without the presence of the U.S. military to maintain control.
White Southerners will ignore the rights guaranteed in the Reconstruction Amendments and treat Black people poorly.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This article from *The Augusta (Georgia) Constitutionalist* quotes Rutherford B. Hayes’s speech after the 1876 presidential election, when Hayes believed he had been defeated by Democrat Samuel J. Tilden.**

I don’t care for myself; and the party, yes, and the country, too, can stand it; but I do care for the poor colored men of the South. I do not fear that business will be greatly disturbed by Mr. Tilden’s election—trade governs itself. . . . As soon as they recover from the [[☃ definition 1]] of defeat business will, I think, go on as usual. . . . If any part of the country suffers or remains at a stand still it will be the South under the new regime. . . . But as I said before, I do pity the poor black men of the South. The result will be that the Southern people will practically treat the constitutional amendments as [[☃ definition 2]], and then the colored man’s fate will be worse than when he was in slavery with a [[☃ definition 3]] master to look after his interests. That is the only reason I regret the news as it is. 

**Which development best supports Hayes’s claim that “the Southern people will practically treat the constitutional amendments as nullities”?

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: things that have no legal authority or force

choices.content: The widespread migration of Black Americans from the southern states to the northern states
The introduction of Jim Crow laws in the South to segregate public facilities and restrict Black men’s right to vote
The rise in Black political power in the years after the Civil War, as Black legislators took their positions in state and federal governments
The secession of Southern states following the election of the Republican candidate, Hayes, to the presidency
